exemplar.title: To what extent do the contrasting identities and aspirations between Jo March and Amy March represent the differing feminist ideologies in the book Little Women?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do the contrasting identities and aspirations between Jo March and Amy March represent the differing feminist ideologies in the book Little Women?

subject.title: English B
subject.description:
**IB English B** is a **Group 2** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **language acquisition through five prescribed themes: identities, experiences, human ingenuity, social organization, and sharing the planet**, with a strong focus on **developing receptive, productive, and interactive communication skills in English as a second language**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Productive skills - writing | 1 hour | 1 hour 15 minutes | 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Receptive skills - listening and reading comprehension | 1 hour 45 minutes | 2 hours | 50% |
| **Individual Oral** | Internal assessment - oral communication | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 25% |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, focused and consistently connected to the analysis throughout the investigation.
The topic is communicated accurately and effectively via a precise title, explicit introduction, and clearly sign-posted chapters.
Findings and chapter headings consistently refer back to the line of inquiry, demonstrating sustained coherence and purpose.
weaknesses: No discrete methodology section and little explicit explanation of source selection or evaluation.
Occasional digressions into broad historical background that are not tightly integrated with the research question.
Minor lapses in phrasing and recurring misspellings (e.g. âLouisa/Louisâ and âLittle Womanâ) detract slightly from clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student demonstrates an excellent grasp of focusâcommunicating the topic clearly and maintaining a tight connection to the research question across chaptersâthe complete absence of an explicit methodology section and only sporadic comments on source selection prevent a topâband score. Occasional offâtopic background and minor errors further justify a score of 5 rather than 6.

strengths: Demonstrates clear and coherent subject knowledge of nineteenth-century gender ideologies and their relation to Joâs and Amyâs character arcs.
Terminology (e.g. âseparate spheres,â âpragmatic/social feminism,â âidealist feminismâ) is generally accurate and appropriately applied.
Primary quotations are used effectively to enrich analysis of the textâs feminist dimensions.
weaknesses: Range of scholarly sources is limited and some secondary materials lack academic weight.
Occasional conceptual imprecision (e.g. conflating âsocialâ and âliberalâ feminism) and factual slips (misspelled authorâs name).
Several citations are incomplete or missing from the reference list, undermining source credibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows a solid grasp of subject content and uses terminology consistently, but uneven source range, conceptual ambiguities, and frequent referencing errors restrict performance to the upper half of the 3-4 band. A score of 4 reflects both coherent knowledge and these notable deficiencies.

strengths: Presents a sustained, logical comparison of Joâs and Amyâs trajectories, linking textual evidence to feminist frameworks.
Arguments progress coherently and quotations are generally interpreted to support claims about gender ideology.
Uses a relevant mix of primary and secondary sources that consistently relate to the research question.
weaknesses: Analysis sometimes remains descriptive, with large block quotations substituting for deeper close reading.
Evaluation of counter-arguments and discussion of limitations is brief or omitted (e.g. treatment of Joâs marriage).
Critical appraisal of source credibility is inconsistent, and the conclusion omits reflection on limitations or further research avenues.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates competent critical thinkingâgood research relevance and logical argumentationâyet it lacks consistently deep analysis and thorough evaluation. The uneven depth of interpretation and minimal critique of sources position the work in the lower half of the 7-9 band, meriting a score of 7.

strengths: Follows the required extended essay structure (title page, contents, chapters, references) in a recognizable and functional manner.
Layout requirements (double spacing, readable font, word count, page numbering) are met and support readability.
weaknesses: Several entries in the contents list lack page numbers and the heading hierarchy is uneven, which disrupts navigation.
Citation and bibliography style is inconsistent and some publication details are missing.
Minor spelling errors in headings and titles reduce overall polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is generally complete and correctly formatted, fulfilling most structural and layout requirements. However, repeated lapses in contents pagination, heading consistency, and referencing prevent a top-band score. A score of 3 reflects a clear but imperfect presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The research questionââTo what extent do the contrasting identities and aspirations between Jo March and Amy March represent the differing feminist ideologies in the book Little Women?ââis clearly articulated, focused and consistently referenced in chapter titles, topic sentences and the findings section.  
â¢  Each analytical section (Jo as idealist; Amy as pragmatist) is overtly linked back to the question, demonstrating coherence between the line of inquiry and the discussion.  
â¢  Although the focus remains sharp, occasional digressions into broad historical background and biographical detail are not always tightly integrated with the stated scope, warranting a score of 5 rather than 6 in the top band.

The essay offers sustained comparison of Joâs and Amyâs trajectories, linking textual evidence to feminist frameworks and drawing conclusions about âidealistâ versus âpragmaticâ feminism.  
â¢  Arguments are generally coherent and progress logically; quotations are interpreted to support claims about gender ideology.  
â¢  Yet analysis sometimes stops at descriptive commentary, large excerpts replace nuanced close reading, and counter-arguments (e.g., Joâs eventual marriage) receive cursory treatment. These factors align with the middle of the 4â6 band (score 5).

The student integrates primary quotations from Little Women and cites several secondary sources (Matteson, Sundgren, University of Sheffield). These references are generally relevant to the argument about feminist ideologies.  
â¢  However, the range is limited (few scholarly sources, some web materials of uneven academic weight) and several historical or interpretive claims remain uncited, reducing overall effectiveness.  
â¢  Long block quotations occasionally substitute for sustained close analysis, signalling that application, while mostly appropriate, is not consistently selective or critically framedâfitting the 3â4 band, awarded 3.

Key layout requirementsâdouble spacing, readable font, title page with word count, and a reference listâare met.  
â¢  Citations and bibliography format are uneven (mix of styles, missing publication details), and some proper nouns/titles are misspelled, but these flaws do not seriously impede comprehension.  
â¢  Given that most formal elements are present and generally correct, the work sits in the 3â4 band; minor but repeated lapses justify a score of 3 rather than 4.

Across chapters the student demonstrates a coherent grasp of nineteenth-century gender ideology, first-wave feminism and Alcottâs biography, weaving these into readings of Joâs and Amyâs character arcs.  
â¢  Explanations of separate-spheres ideology, the Seneca Falls Declaration, and Alcottâs abolitionist background show solid contextual knowledge.  
â¢  Some factual slips (misspelling the authorâs name, misdating or simplifying historical developments) prevent full mastery, but the breadth and generally accurate deployment of information justify placement in the 5â6 band at a score of 5.

The report follows the prescribed EE structureâtitle page, contents, chapters, referencesâbut some required elements are incomplete or inconsistently executed.  
â¢  Several entries in the contents list lack page numbers, and heading hierarchy is uneven, which occasionally disrupts navigation.  
â¢  Despite these issues, the overall structure remains recognizable and functional, earning a solid place in the 1â2 band at the top mark of 2.

The investigation draws on a modest but pertinent set of primary and secondary sources that relate reasonably well to the research question.  
â¢  The novel is read closely, and historical as well as scholarly materials (Matteson, Sundgren, Sheffield) are employed to frame the analysis.  
â¢  Nevertheless, the scope is limited (few peer-reviewed literary studies, some citations incomplete) and there is little critical appraisal of source credibility, placing the work comfortably in the 4â6 band with a mid-range score of 5.

The studentâs title, opening paragraph and clearly sign-posted chapters communicate the focus on feminist ideologies in Little Women with clarity. A precise, explicit research question is stated verbatim in both the title page and introduction, and every subsequent chapter signposts its relevance to this question.  
â¢  The purposeâto compare Joâs and Amyâs contrasting identities/aspirations as embodiments of distinct feminist positionsâis stated explicitly and revisited in the findings.  
â¢  Minor lapses (e.g., recurring misspelling of âLouisa/Louisâ and âLittle Womanâ) slightly detract from polish, but they do not undermine the readerâs understanding of the topic or its appropriateness.  
Given the overall clarity and sustained focus, the work fits securely in the 5â6 band, though the small inaccuracies and occasional vagueness in phrasing keep it at the lower end of that band (5).

The discussion is generally logical, moving from context to character studies to findings, and it returns explicitly to the research question.  
â¢  Reasoned argument is apparent, but evaluation of alternative interpretations (e.g., differing scholarly views on Joâs marriage, limits of first-wave feminism) is brief, and some assertions about reader reception or historical impact are left unsupported.  
â¢  Occasional repetition and reliance on summary cause minor lapses in focus, keeping the work in the lower half of the 4â6 band (score 4).

No discrete methodology section is provided, and the essay offers only sporadic comments on how sources were located or selected.  
â¢  While some scholarly and primary texts are cited, there is little explicit explanation of why these particular works were chosen, how their reliability was assessed or what alternative approaches were considered.  
â¢  The absence of a transparent account of research methods, criteria for source evaluation or acknowledgment of limitations points to a largely *implicit* rather than *explicit* methodology, characteristic of the 1â2 band. A top score in this band (2) is merited because there is indirect evidence of purposeful source use, even if the process is not fully articulated.

Key conceptsââseparate spheres,â âpragmatic/social feminism,â âidealist feminism,â âVictorian gender normsââare used regularly and mostly accurately.  
â¢  The student explains terms such as âtomboyâ, âabolitionistâ, and âfirst-wave feminismâ, showing functional command of subject vocabulary.  
â¢  Nonetheless, occasional imprecision (e.g., conflating âliberalâ and âsocialâ feminism, inconsistent use of âVictorianâ versus ânineteenth-centuryâ) and grammatical slips limit full consistency, situating the work towards the upper end of the 3â4 band (score 4) rather than the highest band.

annotations.comment: The terms âsocial feminismâ and âliberal feminismâ are conflated. Define each concept precisely to avoid conceptual ambiguity.
The conclusion effectively summarizes your argument but omits discussion of limitations and avenues for further research, which would strengthen critical reflection.
The reference list shows inconsistent citation formats and omits key sources cited in the text (e.g., Fraisse & Perrot, Errazuris). Standardize style and include all references.
Effective use of primary quotations here illustrates Amyâs pragmatic perspective and enriches the textual analysis of her character.
The stated objective outlines broad aims but does not explicitly link back to the research question. Clarify how these goals will directly address your inquiry.
Several chapter entries in the table of contents lack page numbers, which impedes navigation and impacts structural completeness.
The discussion of 19th-century gender roles is informative but lacks explicit connection to your research question. Tie each contextual point back to how it informs your analysis of Jo and Amy.
The University of Sheffield source is used without discussing its scholarly reliability or your criteria for selection. Reflect on why this web resource is appropriate.
The student correctly provides a word count on the title page, fulfilling the formatting requirement for an extended essay.
The title misnames the primary text as âLittle Womanâ rather than âLittle Women,â which may confuse readers and undermines precision in presenting the research focus.
The sentence beginning âWhich she achievesâ¦â is a fragment that disrupts clarity. Combine it with the preceding sentence to improve coherence and flow.
The authorâs name is misspelled as âLouis May Alcottâ instead of âLouisa May Alcottâ in this paragraph, undermining subject knowledge and attention to detail.
The chapter heading repeats the misspelling âLouis May Alcott,â creating inconsistency. Ensure the authorâs name is rendered correctly throughout.
The indentation and hierarchy of headings in the contents list are inconsistent, making the structure harder to interpret at a glance.
The quotation from Fraisse and Perrot (2000:32) is not included in the reference list. Add the full bibliographic entry to validate this citation.
Key biographical details (e.g., birth date and family background) are presented without citation. Provide sources to substantiate these factual claims.
You quote Fraisse and Perrot without including them in the references. This omission weakens source credibilityâadd the full reference to your bibliography.
The quotation from Sundgren (2022) is presented uncritically. Evaluate how this interpretation aligns or conflicts with other scholarly perspectives.
This sentence lacks a clear subject and the transition is abrupt, which disrupts readability. Rephrase to improve flow and coherence.
Frequent informal bolded transitions (âButâ) weaken the academic tone. Use more formal connectors to strengthen argumentation.
Character descriptions are drawn mainly from summary rather than scholarship. Integrate additional peer-reviewed analyses to deepen your subject knowledge.
The discussion of reader pressure on Joâs marriage is asserted without evidence from reviews or secondary studies. Support this claim with appropriate scholarly sources.
Although the primary evidence is strong, the argument would benefit from engaging with additional scholarly interpretations of Amyâs development.
The findings section provides a focused synthesis that directly addresses the research question, demonstrating coherence between inquiry and conclusion.
This block of text is too dense and heavily narrative. Break into shorter paragraphs and focus on critical analysis rather than summary.
